findings.justification: The $_GET['filename'] parameter is passed to set_filename() with only wp_unslash() applied and no sanitization to remove path traversal characters (e.g., '../'), allowing an attacker with a valid nonce to specify an arbitrary filename path.
The $_POST['filename'] parameter is passed to set_filename() with only wp_unslash() and no path sanitization, allowing a privileged user to set a filename containing path traversal sequences.

download_export_file() relies solely on nonce verification (wp_verify_nonce) to gate access without re-checking export_allowed() at execution time; the capability check occurs only at hook-registration time in the constructor, so if a user's privileges are revoked after page load, the check is bypassed.

The $_POST['columns'] and $_POST['selected_columns'] values are passed to exporter methods without verifying they are arrays, so a non-array value (e.g., a string) could cause unexpected behavior or errors in downstream processing.

Product IDs split from $_POST['export_product_ids'] are not cast to integers before being passed to set_product_ids_to_export(), so non-numeric string values in the comma-separated list could propagate downstream and potentially cause issues if the exporter uses them in database queries without type enforcement.
